Aspects of Danceability¶

Rachel Garcia¶

Tuesday, April 28, 2026¶

Outline¶

  1. Background & Motivation
  2. The Data
  3. Exploratory Analysis
  4. Methods
  5. Results
  6. Example
  7. Conclusion and Reflection

Background¶

In the aftermath of the pandemic, people are feeling mass amounts of disconnect. This mental impact has left people more willing to find community than ever. Dance is a way to connect people from any background.

The Question¶

What audio features make a song danceable?¶

Why this matters: More danceable music = more dancing = stronger communities!¶

The Formula¶

This analysis resulted in a few key findings for what can systematically create a danceble track.

  1. High valence or 'feel-good' vibe throughout
  1. High percentage of speechiness - indicating more rap, hip-hop, reggaeton, trap, etc.
  1. Strong, consistent, driving rhythm

The Dataset¶

  • High popularity songs from Spotify's API, as sourced from Kaggle
  • 1686 tracks, 29 columns
  • Target variable: Danceability (0-1 scale)

Summary Statistics¶

  • Mean danceability is 0.65 -- songs are relatively danceable
  • Max danceability is 0.979

Exploratory Scatterplots¶

Choosing some random variables to see if there is any variables with strong predictive power

In [6]:
sns.scatterplot(Spotify_DF, x = "valence", y = "danceability", alpha = 0.4, color = 'green')
Out[6]:
<Axes: xlabel='valence', ylabel='danceability'>
Out[6]:

Slight positive slope, but ainly random cloud, suggesting potential predictive power

In [7]:
sns.scatterplot(Spotify_DF, x = "tempo", y = "danceability", alpha = 0.4, color = 'red')
Out[7]:
<Axes: xlabel='tempo', ylabel='danceability'>
Out[7]:

Surprisingly not much to pull from this, considering tempo is often associated with dancing

Since these and others provided no singular stand out variable, we will let Subset Selection do the work

Methods Overview¶

  1. Best Subset Selection
  2. OLS Regression
  3. Decision Tree Regressor
  4. GridSearchCV
  5. Evaluation

track_popularity     0.0041      0.000     10.429      0.000       0.003       0.005

Prob(Omnibus):                  0.000   Jarque-Bera (JB):               32.816
Skew:                          -0.337   Prob(JB):                     7.49e-08
Kurtosis:                       2.891   Cond. No.                     2.27e+06
==============================================================================

Notes:
[1] R² is computed without centering (uncentered) since the model does not contain a constant.
[2] Standard Errors assume that the covariance matrix of the errors is correctly specified.
[3] The condition number is large, 2.27e+06. This might indicate that there are
strong multicollinearity or other numerical problems.

  • $R^2 = 0.957$: the model explains 95.7% of danceability variation

  • Speechiness coefficient = 0.3524

  • Valence coefficient = 0.2429

  • Energy coefficient = -0.1938

  • All variables have a $p<0.001$

GridSearchCV Tuning¶

In [16]:
#importing required package
from sklearn.model_selection import GridSearchCV

# our parameter limits for each end to prevent overfitting
param_grid = {
    "tree__max_depth": [3,5,7],
    "tree__min_samples_leaf": [5,10,15]
}

grid_search = GridSearchCV(
    pipeline, param_grid, cv=5, scoring = "r2")
grid_search.fit(X_train,y_train)

print("Best parameters:", grid_search.best_params_)

Best parameters: {'tree__max_depth': 5, 'tree__min_samples_leaf': 5}

Max depth of 5 so the tree doesn't go too deep and overfit

Min leaf samples of 5 so each leaf needs 5 datapoints

Visualizing the Regression Tree¶

In [18]:
from sklearn.tree import plot_tree

plt.figure(figsize=(40, 15))
plot_tree(
    regressor,
    feature_names = ["energy", "loudness", "valence", "time_signature", "speechiness", "track_popularity", "duration_ms"],
    filled=True,
    fontsize=10
)
plt.title("Decision Tree Structure")
plt.show()
Out[18]:
  • Root split on valence, most important variable
  • Speechiness appearing early as well
  • Note the missing leaves, min_samples_leaf doing its job

MSE = 0.0176 suggests that our predicitions are only off by about 0.017 on a 0-1 scale

Example: Most Danceable Song¶

By the actual data: Ice Ice Baby has the highest danceability score of 0.979

When ran through the decision tree finds that noviogangsta < 3 has the highest danceability score of 0.8112 (actual danceability of 0.8160)

The difference between these two suggests that Ice Ice Baby's danceability is an outlier, the raw score much higher than it's actual features would alone predict

Results Summary¶

Valence strongest predictor, emotional tone matters most

Speechiness rap and hip-hop style vocal content is a driver

Time Signature regular beat structure aids

Energy a negative coefficient suggests that high intensity does not imply highly danceable

Conclusion¶

  • Dance builds community in a time of disconnect
  • Recipe for a danceable song: positive tone + vocal presence + strong rhythm
  • Artists and producers now have a databacked roadmap

Reflection¶

What worked well:

  1. Subset selection guiding the tree
  2. Strong MSE of 0.0176
  3. Two models corroborate each other

What I struggled with:

  1. Pipeline and GridSearchCV syntax
  2. Not overfitting the tree
  3. Jupyter Notebook Slides!

What I would do differently:

  1. Make genre, sub-genre, playlist genre, and other variables numeric ordinal variables to see if they have input
  2. Try other models for comparison, maybe Random Forest
  3. Explore within each genre to see if the results differe upon closer inspection

Citations¶

Corning, Anna. “Disconnected but Hopeful: What a New Survey Reveals about Civic Life in America.” National Civic League, National Civic League, 6 Oct. 2025.

Mohammed, Ahmed. spotify-music-dataset. Version 1 of 1. 2025.

Sawatsky, Melissa. “Shall We Dance? Building Community and Bridging Cultures.” Culturally Modified, 2019.
